title: Sperm Analysis Calculator – Male Fertility Assessment
tagline: Evaluate sperm quality parameters against WHO standards and receive a comprehensive fertility score and grade.
intro: Enter your semen analysis results to calculate total sperm count, motile sperm count, fertility score, and quality grade based on WHO 6th edition reference values.

error.invalid: Please enter valid numeric values for all fields.

about.heading: About the Sperm Analysis Calculator
about.body:
A semen analysis is the cornerstone of male fertility evaluation, and this calculator transforms raw laboratory measurements into a comprehensive fertility assessment based on the World Health Organization's sixth-edition laboratory manual — the current global standard for semen examination published in 2021. The calculator evaluates seven parameters: sperm concentration, semen volume, progressive motility, normal morphology, sperm vitality, pH level, and liquefaction time, and combines them into a weighted fertility score and an overall quality grade.

Sperm concentration, expressed as millions per milliliter (million/mL), describes how many sperm cells are present in each unit of ejaculate. The WHO lower reference limit is 15 million/mL; values below this threshold are classified as oligospermia. Combined with semen volume, concentration yields the total sperm count per ejaculate — the actual number of sperm cells available to reach and fertilize an egg. Volume alone matters too: a volume below 1.5 mL reduces the buffering capacity of seminal fluid and exposes more sperm to the acidic vaginal environment. Total sperm count per ejaculate should ideally exceed 39 million.

Progressive motility describes the percentage of sperm swimming actively forward. Only progressively motile sperm can travel through the cervical mucus and uterine cavity to reach the fallopian tube where fertilization occurs. The WHO reference limit is 32%; below this level, impaired sperm transport becomes a significant cause of subfertility. Normal morphology, assessed by strict Kruger criteria, measures the proportion of sperm with normally shaped heads, midpieces, and tails. The reference limit of 4% may seem low, but morphology is a strong predictor of fertilization rates in assisted reproductive technology (ART) procedures.

Vitality (percentage of live sperm) distinguishes between immotile-but-alive sperm and non-viable cells. A vitality below 58% suggests excessive cell death and may indicate epididymal dysfunction or oxidative stress. The pH of semen, normally between 7.2 and 8.0, reflects the balance between alkaline prostatic secretions and slightly acidic seminal vesicle fluid; an abnormal pH can signal infection or anatomical abnormalities. Liquefaction time reflects how quickly the gel-like ejaculate becomes liquid, a process driven by prostate enzymes. Incomplete liquefaction after 60 minutes can trap sperm and impair motility.

This calculator uses a weighted fertility scoring algorithm: progressive motility contributes 30% of the score (reflecting its strong correlation with natural conception rates), sperm count and normal morphology each contribute 25%, and vitality contributes 20%. Each parameter is normalized against its WHO reference value so that reaching the reference limit yields a base score of 50, and values twice the limit yield a score of 100. This approach produces a single 0–100 score and a quality grade — Excellent (90–100), Good (70–89), Fair (50–69), or Poor (0–49) — that summarizes overall fertility potential. The results are intended for educational use and should be discussed with a reproductive medicine specialist before any clinical decisions are made.

examples.heading: Sperm analysis examples
examples.intro: These examples show how different parameter combinations affect the fertility score and quality grade.

items.input: Count 45 M/mL, Volume 3.5 mL, Motility 55%, Morphology 8%, Vitality 75%
items.note: All parameters above WHO limits; high fertility potential suitable for natural conception.

items.input: Count 12 M/mL, Volume 2 mL, Motility 25%, Morphology 3%, Vitality 60%
items.note: Multiple parameters below WHO reference limits; likely requires ART evaluation.

items.input: Count 120 M/mL, Volume 4.5 mL, Motility 75%, Morphology 15%, Vitality 85%
items.note: All parameters well above limits; excellent fertility potential, very favorable for natural conception.

items.input: Count 20 M/mL, Volume 2.5 mL, Motility 35%, Morphology 5%, Vitality 62%
items.note: Parameters near or slightly above WHO limits; moderate fertility potential, lifestyle optimization recommended.

howto.heading: How to use the Sperm Analysis Calculator
steps: Obtain a complete semen analysis from a certified laboratory. The sample should be collected after 2–7 days of sexual abstinence and analyzed within 1 hour of collection.

Enter each value exactly as reported: sperm concentration in million/mL, semen volume in mL, progressive motility as a percentage, normal morphology percentage, vitality percentage, pH as a decimal, and liquefaction time in minutes.
Click 'Calculate Sperm Analysis' to see derived values (total count, motile count, normal count), the 0–100 fertility score, and the quality grade.
Compare each parameter against the WHO reference values displayed below the result to identify which specific parameters fall below clinical thresholds.
Share the results with a reproductive medicine specialist or urologist for a full clinical interpretation and personalized treatment recommendations.

items.q: What WHO edition does this calculator use?
items.a: This calculator is based on the World Health Organization Laboratory Manual for the Examination and Processing of Human Semen, 6th Edition (2021). The reference limits reflect the 5th percentile values from fertile men who achieved pregnancy within 12 months of unprotected intercourse.

items.q: Why does morphology have such a low reference limit (4%)?
items.a: Normal morphology is assessed by strict Kruger criteria, where any sperm with minor head, midpiece, or tail defects is classified as abnormal. As a result, even fertile men typically have fewer than 10% normal forms. The 4% threshold represents the 5th percentile of fertile men — values below this are associated with significantly lower natural fertilization rates and IVF success rates.

items.q: Can a man with parameters below WHO limits still father a child?
items.a: Yes. WHO reference values are statistical lower limits derived from fertile populations, not absolute cutoffs for sterility. Many men with parameters below these limits conceive naturally, while some men with normal parameters may struggle with fertility due to other factors. The calculator's fertility score provides a probabilistic assessment, not a definitive judgment.

items.q: How is the fertility score calculated?
items.a: The score uses a weighted algorithm that normalizes each parameter against its WHO reference value. Values at the reference limit score 50; values twice the limit score 100. Progressive motility contributes 30%, sperm count 25%, normal morphology 25%, and vitality 20% to the final 0–100 score. pH and liquefaction time are evaluated separately for reference but are not included in the main score weighting.

items.q: How much can lifestyle changes improve sperm parameters?
items.a: Sperm production (spermatogenesis) takes approximately 74 days, so lifestyle changes take at least 3 months to improve semen parameters. Evidence-based improvements include quitting smoking, reducing alcohol intake, achieving a healthy BMI, avoiding heat exposure to the testes, managing stress, and ensuring adequate intake of antioxidants such as vitamin C, vitamin E, zinc, and selenium.

items.q: Should a single semen analysis be taken as definitive?
items.a: No. Sperm parameters show significant biological variability from one sample to the next. Clinical guidelines recommend at least two analyses 2–4 weeks apart for a reliable assessment. Temporary factors such as recent fever, illness, stress, or alcohol use can transiently suppress parameters. If the first analysis shows abnormalities, a confirmatory test is strongly recommended before pursuing further evaluation.
